confirmed.claim: Prentice's fourth criterion (testing whether the surrogate fully captures the treatment effect via a regression coefficient test) is asymmetric: it can only reject a poor surrogate, not affirmatively validate a good one, because failing to reject the null (that treatment effect on the true endpoint is fully explained by the surrogate) is not proof of validity — only absence of evidence against it.
confirmed.quote: it can only be used to reject a poor surrogate, and not to validate a good surrogate
confirmed.evidence: The claim is directly and precisely supported by the primary source. Fetching pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25372337 (Alonso et al., "Statistical Validation of Surrogate Endpoints: Another Look at the Prentice Criterion and Other Criteria") returns the exact statement: "Testing such a null hypothesis has been criticized in the literature since it can only be used to reject a poor surrogate, and not to validate a good surrogate." This matches the supporting quote given verbatim in substance and confirms the asymmetric-logic framing the claim asserts (failure to reject the null that the treatment effect on the true endpoint is captured by the surrogate is not proof of validity, only absence of evidence against invalidity — standard Neyman-Pearson logic, correctly applied here). A supplementary web search corroborates with near-identical independent phrasing: "The fourth criterion is useful to reject a poor surrogate, but it is not suitable to validate a good surrogate (i.e., a non-significant result may always be attributable to a lack of statistical power)... the result of the statistical test... cannot prove that the effect of the treatment on the true endpoint is fully captured by the surrogate." No contradicting source was found; rather, this is presented as the settled motivation in the surrogate-endpoint literature for developing alternative approaches (e.g., equivalence-hypothesis reformulations, meta-analytic/surrogate-index methods) precisely because this asymmetry is accepted as a known limitation, not disputed. The claim is not overreach, not outdated (it is a structural statistical-logic point, unaffected by field advances — indeed it is cited as the reason later methods like equivalence testing and the Athey et al. surrogate index were developed), and the source (a peer-reviewed statistical methods paper specifically on Prentice-criterion validation) is appropriately authoritative for this claim's strength.

confirmed.claim: The authors propose reformulating surrogate validation as an equivalence-testing problem (rather than a standard null-hypothesis significance test), under which validation is claimed only when a null hypothesis of non-equivalence/inadequacy is rejected — inverting the burden of proof so 'validated' requires an affirmative statistical demonstration.
confirmed.quote: the surrogate endpoint is statistically validated when the null hypothesis is rejected
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the PubMed abstract directly (pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25372337, "Statistical Validation of Surrogate Endpoints: Another Look at the Prentice Criterion and Other Criteria"). Full abstract confirms the claim verbatim: the standard NHST on Prentice's 4th-criterion regression parameter (H0: beta=0) "can only be used to reject a poor surrogate, and not to validate a good surrogate." The authors then formulate "an equivalence hypothesis... that the parameter is equivalent to zero" as the ALTERNATIVE hypothesis, "so that the surrogate endpoint is statistically validated when the null hypothesis is rejected." This is exactly the equivalence-testing reformulation (TOST-style, burden-of-proof inversion) the claim describes, and the supporting quote given ("the surrogate endpoint is statistically validated when the null hypothesis is rejected") is an exact substring of the abstract. A follow-up search for critiques of this specific equivalence-testing approach for the Prentice criterion turned up no contradiction — surrounding literature critiques the Prentice criteria generally (criterion 4 is hard to satisfy, criteria are necessary-not-sufficient) but does not dispute that this paper proposes the equivalence-test inversion. Source is the primary paper itself via its own abstract, which matches the claim's strength (a claim about what one paper proposes, not a claim about field consensus).

confirmed.claim: Prentice's (1989) original surrogate-endpoint validation criteria are only mutually equivalent (i.e., the criteria collapse into a single consistent definition) when both the surrogate and the true endpoint are binary variables — a condition that fails for continuous or time-to-event endpoints, which is the more common case in practice.
confirmed.quote: The validation of surrogate endpoints has been studied by Prentice (1989). He presented a definition as well as a set of criteria, which are equivalent only if the surrogate and true endpoints are binary.
confirmed.evidence: The claim is directly and verbatim supported by its cited primary source. I confirmed via the actual Oxford Academic journal page (Buyse, Molenberghs, Burzykowski, Renard & Geys, "The Validation of Surrogate Endpoints in Meta-analyses of Randomized Experiments," Biostatistics 1(1):49-67, 2000 — not just the ResearchGate mirror) that the abstract states verbatim: "The validation of surrogate endpoints has been studied by Prentice (1989). He presented a definition as well as a set of criteria, which are equivalent only if the surrogate and true endpoints are binary." This is a landmark, heavily-cited paper in the surrogate-endpoint literature, and it is the correct primary source for this specific claim (note: Prentice 1989 itself, Statistics in Medicine 8(4):431-440, is the original criteria paper; the binary-equivalence observation is the widely-cited finding of Buyse/Molenberghs's 2000 re-analysis of Prentice's framework — the claim's sourcing is accurate on this point). Independent web search corroborates: multiple summaries (including of Freedman et al. 1992's 'proportion explained' critique and Buyse & Molenberghs 1998's individual/trial-level replacement measures) repeat the same equivalence-only-for-binary characterization, and note that Buyse/Molenberghs's original framework was restricted to jointly-normal or jointly-binary cases specifically because the equivalence breaks down otherwise, with later literature extending to mixed binary/continuous and time-to-event cases — consistent with the claim's assertion that non-binary (continuous, time-to-event) endpoints are the harder, more common real-world case. No contradicting or qualifying source was found. The claim's added clause that continuous/time-to-event endpoints are 'the more common case in practice' is a reasonable, factually uncontroversial extrapolation (clinical trials commonly use survival/time-to-event or continuous biomarker endpoints) rather than a misattribution to the source. The finding is a technical/historical statistical fact, not a fast-moving empirical result, so its age (per a 2000 paper analyzing an 1989 paper) does not make it outdated — it is still cited by current (post-2020) surrogate-validation literature in the same terms.

confirmed.claim: The trial-level measure is explicitly framed as a prediction tool: it allows a builder to predict the effect of an intervention on the (lagging) true/north-star outcome from its observed effect on the (leading) surrogate, which is the core mechanism a surrogate-index construction method must deliver.
confirmed.quote: prediction of the effect of treatment upon the true endpoint, given its observed effect upon the surrogate endpoint
confirmed.evidence: The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source (Buyse, Molenberghs, Burzykowski, Renard & Geys, "The Validation of Surrogate Endpoints in Meta-analyses of Randomized Experiments," Biostatistics 1(1):49-67, 2000 — the seminal trial-level/meta-analytic surrogate validation paper). Independent confirmation via WebSearch and a second WebFetch (academic.oup.com abstract page, since the ResearchGate URL itself 403'd) both reproduce language matching the supporting quote nearly verbatim: the trial-level measure "naturally leads to the prediction of the effect of treatment upon the true endpoint, given its observed effect upon the surrogate endpoint." This is the paper's own stated purpose for the trial-level (as opposed to individual-level) surrogacy measure — it is not a misread or extrapolation of an incidental aside. The claim's final clause ("which is the core mechanism a surrogate-index construction method must deliver") is the reviewer's own connective framing linking this clinical-trials prediction goal to the general surrogate-index construction task, not a misattribution to the source — and it is a reasonable characterization, since predicting the lagging/true outcome from the leading/surrogate effect is exactly the shared objective across the clinical (Prentice/Buyse) and econometric (Athey et al.) surrogate literatures. Source quality matches claim strength: peer-reviewed Biostatistics (Oxford Academic) foundational paper, still cited as the canonical trial-level framework in a 2025 arXiv tutorial found in the same search, so it is not outdated for methodological purposes despite its 2000 publication date. No search result surfaced any contradiction or heavy qualification of this specific framing. This is not a marketing claim, press release, or forum speculation.

confirmed.claim: Buyse and Molenberghs (1998) extend Prentice's surrogate-endpoint criteria into a quantitative framework requiring two meta-analytic-scale quantities: Proportion of treatment Effect explained (PE) by the surrogate, and Relative Effect (RE) of treatment on the true endpoint versus the surrogate, plus an adjusted association (R^2 / gamma_Z) between surrogate and true endpoint after conditioning on treatment.
confirmed.quote: estimate what percentage of the treatment's effect on the true endpoint is explained by the surrogate
confirmed.evidence: Claim confirmed by both the PubMed primary source and corroborating secondary sources. The PubMed abstract for Buyse & Molenberghs (1998), "Criteria for the Validation of Surrogate Endpoints in Randomized Experiments," Biometrics 54:1014-1029, explicitly defines all three quantities named in the claim: PE ("the proportion of the effect of Z on T that is explained by S"), RE ("the effect of Z on T relative to that of Z on S"), and gamma_Z ("the association between S and T after adjustment for Z" — an R^2-type adjusted-association measure). The abstract frames these as the tools for assessing whether a surrogate is "perfect," stating a perfect surrogate requires perfect individual-level association (gamma_Z) after treatment adjustment AND RE = 1 at the population level — i.e., exactly the "two meta-analytic-scale quantities plus an adjusted association" structure the claim describes. Independent corroboration: a WebSearch summary (SciRP reference entry, ResearchGate, Biostatistics journal citation) confirms the paper is B&M (1998), Biometrics 54:1014-1029, and that it extends/responds to Prentice (1989) and Freedman, Graubard & Schatzkin (1992), the papers that established PE and the earlier qualitative Prentice criteria — consistent with the claim's framing of B&M as extending Prentice's criteria into a quantitative framework. One minor nuance not fatal to the claim: B&M's paper is somewhat critical of PE's practical instability (requiring the treatment effect on T be "massively significant," a rare situation) and their own preferred contribution emphasizes RE and gamma_Z over PE — but the claim does not assert PE is unproblematic, only that PE and RE are the two quantities plus adjusted association, which the abstract itself explicitly lays out. The supporting quote in the claim ("estimate what percentage of the treatment's effect...explained by the surrogate") matches the PE definition verbatim in substance. Source is the primary paper itself (PubMed abstract), appropriate for a claim of this specificity; no contradicting or qualifying source was found; the characterization is standard and uncontested in the surrogate-endpoint literature (still cited this way as of the most recent 2026 papers found, e.g., an arXiv 2026 paper on binary surrogate validation still references this PE/RE/individual-association framework as foundational).

confirmed.claim: The paper identifies a specific failure condition: the Proportion of Effect (PE) statistic is only estimable/reliable when the treatment has a very large effect on the true endpoint -- a condition the authors call rare -- meaning PE-based validation breaks down for typical, modest treatment effects.
confirmed.quote: can only be verified if the treatment has a massively significant effect on the true endpoint, a rare situation
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the primary source (PubMed 9840970, Buyse/Molenberghs et al., "Criteria for the validation of surrogate endpoints in randomized experiments," Biometrics/Stat Med, 1998) directly. The abstract contains the exact supporting quote verbatim: "This condition can only be verified if the treatment has a massively significant effect on the true endpoint, a rare situation" — stated specifically about the Proportion of Effect (PE) statistic (proportion of treatment effect on the true endpoint T explained by surrogate S), following the requirement that PE's lower confidence bound exceed a threshold (e.g., 0.5-0.75). A corroborating web search independently returned the same passage, attributing it to the paper's critique of the Freedman-Graubard-Schatzkin (1992) PE approach, and confirms this is a foundational, still-cited critique in the surrogate-endpoint methodology literature (e.g., referenced in a 2023 Annual Reviews piece on surrogate endpoints), not an outdated or superseded claim. The claim under review is a faithful, non-overreaching paraphrase: 'only estimable/reliable given a very large effect, a rare condition' maps directly onto 'can only be verified if...massively significant effect...a rare situation,' and 'breaks down for typical, modest effects' is the direct logical converse of 'requires massive effect,' not an added inference beyond what the source states. Source quality (peer-reviewed methods paper, primary source, directly fetched) matches the claim's specificity and strength. No contradicting or qualifying source was found.

confirmed.claim: Practical validation of a surrogate under this framework demands large sample sizes (or pooled/meta-analytic data across multiple trials), not a single small dataset, limiting when a builder can credibly declare a proxy metric validated.
confirmed.quote: validation of surrogate endpoints...require[s] large numbers of observations
confirmed.evidence: The quote is accurate, not cherry-picked or misread. Buyse & Molenberghs 1998 (Biometrics, "Criteria for the validation of surrogate endpoints in randomized experiments") abstract states verbatim: "the validation of surrogate endpoints is shown to require large numbers of observations" for the validation "to be of practical value" — this directly matches the claim's supporting quote. Follow-up literature (which built the now-standard meta-analytic surrogacy-validation framework, e.g. the ScienceDirect meta-analytic-framework paper, the 2019 PMC6649812 systematic review, and a 2025 arXiv tutorial on joint-modeling surrogate validation) confirms the field's consensus: an "informative validation of a surrogate endpoint will typically require large numbers of observations coming from several trials," precisely because the meta-analytic approach was developed to overcome the statistical limits of single-trial evaluation (trial-level R² needs many trial units, not just many patients, for precision). Separately, work using a single very large trial (LEADER, n=9,340, PMC8139150) as a substitute for multi-trial pooling is presented as a workaround/exception to the standard requirement, not a refutation of it — it still requires an unusually large sample, consistent with the claim's "large sample sizes (or pooled data)" framing. The claim's added inference ("limiting when a builder can credibly declare a proxy validated") is a reasonable, non-overreaching restatement of the same point, not an extrapolation into unrelated territory. The paper is foundational (Biometrics, heavily cited primary methodological source) and, despite being from 1998, remains the basis of current practice as shown by papers from 2019–2025 still applying and extending its framework — so it is not stale/outdated for this claim. No credible source found disputing or substantially undercutting the large-sample/meta-analytic requirement; the only nuance is that a single sufficiently large trial can sometimes substitute for multi-trial pooling, which is already accommodated by the claim's phrasing.

confirmed.claim: The paper's proposed exclusion criterion is proven optimal in a specific sense: it is both sufficient (satisfying it guarantees no paradox) and 'almost necessary' (failing it means a data-generating process consistent with the same observed data exists that does exhibit the paradox), and unlike Prentice-style criteria it is directly testable from observed data alone, without unverifiable distributional/confounder assumptions.
confirmed.quote: if the conditions are satisfied, the surrogate paradox is guaranteed to be absent while if the conditions fail, there exists a data generating process with surrogate paradox that can generate the same observed data
confirmed.evidence: Fetched the arxiv abstract page for 1607.05454 ("Novel Criteria to Exclude the Surrogate Paradox and Their Optimalities," Yin, Liu, Geng, Luo — published Scandinavian Journal of Statistics, 2020) and cross-checked via WebSearch. The abstract independently confirms all three parts of the claim, not just the single quoted sentence: (1) sufficiency — "if the conditions are satisfied, the surrogate paradox is guaranteed to be absent"; (2) almost-necessity — "if the conditions fail, there exists a data generating process with surrogate paradox that can generate the same observed data"; (3) testability without unverifiable assumptions — the abstract states the conditions "are testable since they only involve observed data" and that they "capture all the information in the observed data to exclude the surrogate paradox rather than relying on unverifiable distributional assumptions," explicitly contrasting this with prior (Prentice-style) approaches. A search result also notes the paper shows "Prentice's criteria for surrogates can be strengthened to remedy some weaknesses," confirming the Prentice contrast. No credible source disputes or qualifies the optimality/testability claims; no contradicting critique or counterexample turned up in search. Full PDF text extraction failed (binary/corrupted), so verification rests on the abstract/secondary summaries rather than the full proof, but the abstract's claims are essentially verbatim what the reviewed claim asserts, source is a peer-reviewed primary paper matching the claim's strength, and the result is a mathematical/statistical claim not subject to field-speed staleness.

confirmed.claim: Equivalently, in the normal-normal model the shrinkage weight is precision-ratio driven: w = (1/τ0²)/(1/τ0²+1/σ²), and posterior precision is additive (1/τ1² = 1/τ0²+1/σ²). Since σ² (variance of the own-data mean) shrinks as sample size n grows (σ²=σ0²/n), the own-data weight (1-w) rises smoothly toward 1 as own-data variance falls below the prior's τ0² — confirming the general shrinkage-weight form alpha = tau²/(tau²+se²) cited in the research grounding.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched and extracted the full text of the primary source (Peter Hoff, "Shrinkage and empirical Bayes," Duke stats course notes, Feb 2025) via pdftotext. The quoted equations appear verbatim in Section 2 ("Admissible linear shrinkage estimators"): "1/τ1² = 1/τ0² + 1/σ²" and "θ1 = [1/τ0²/(1/τ0²+1/σ²)]θ0 + [1/σ²/(1/τ0²+1/σ²)]x ≡ wθ0 + (1-w)x" -- an exact match to the claim's supporting quote, confirming the quote is not fabricated or misread. I independently re-derived the algebra: w = (1/τ0²)/(1/τ0²+1/σ²) simplifies to σ²/(σ²+τ0²), so (1-w) = τ0²/(τ0²+σ²). As σ²→0, (1-w)→1, confirming the claim's core assertion that own-data weight rises smoothly toward 1 as own-data variance shrinks -- and (1-w)=τ0²/(τ0²+σ²) is exactly the form "alpha=tau²/(tau²+se²)" the claim says it confirms (tau²=τ0² prior variance, se²=σ² own-data variance). This matches standard James-Stein/empirical-Bayes literature (Efron-Morris 1973, also in the same document's Section 6.2) and is uncontested standard statistical theory. The one non-quoted addition -- "σ²=σ0²/n" for a sample mean -- is a textbook fact (variance of a sample mean) not proven in this exact section of the source but consistent with the source's own framing elsewhere ("imagine that each Xj is the sample mean ... from experiment j," and Section 7's explicit n-observations-per-group construction). This addition is correctly stated, uncontroversial, and doesn't misrepresent what the quote itself proves -- it's an unremarkable auxiliary fact bolted onto a verified derivation, not an overreach that changes the claim's substance. No arithmetic errors, no sign-flip errors, no contradiction found in the derivation or in a search of the surrounding econometrics/EB literature.

confirmed.claim: The James-Stein estimator estimates the shrinkage intensity empirically from the data itself (no oracle/prior variance needed) via delta_JS(X) = (1-(p-2)/(X·X))X, i.e. an empirical-Bayes plug-in for the ideal but unobservable oracle shrinkage tau²/(tau²+1) — demonstrating a concrete, general method for deriving a data-driven authority weight without a preset threshold.
confirmed.quote: δJS(X) = (τ²/(τˆ²+1))X = (1 − (p−2)/X·X)X. This estimator is called the James-Stein estimator.
confirmed.evidence: Fetched and read the primary source directly (Peter Hoff, "Shrinkage and empirical Bayes," Duke Stat course notes, dated Feb 20, 2025, pages 13-15). The source's own derivation matches the claim almost verbatim: for X ~ N_p(theta, I) with theta ~ N_p(0, tau^2 I), the oracle Bayes estimator is delta_tau2(X) = (tau^2/(tau^2+1))X, requiring the unobservable tau^2. The notes then derive, using ||X||^2 ~ Gamma(p/2, 1/[2(tau^2+1)]) (valid for p>2), that (p-2)/||X||^2 is an unbiased estimator of 1/(tau^2+1), hence 1-(p-2)/||X||^2 unbiasedly estimates tau^2/(tau^2+1). The notes then write explicitly: "delta_JS(X) = (widehat{tau^2/(tau^2+1)})X = (1-(p-2)/||X||^2)X. This estimator is called the James-Stein estimator." This is exactly the claim's formula and exactly its framing ("empirical-Bayes plug-in for the ideal but unobservable oracle shrinkage tau^2/(tau^2+1)," "no oracle/prior variance needed" for tau^2). The math is internally consistent, is standard and long-established (Efron-Morris 1975/James-Stein 1961 lineage, echoed in essentially every empirical-Bayes textbook treatment), and is not the kind of claim that goes stale. One minor omission in the claim (not a misrepresentation): the derivation explicitly requires p>2 and assumes the noise variance sigma^2=1 is known — the claim only says no *prior*-variance oracle is needed, which is literally true and doesn't claim sigma^2 is also estimated, so this isn't a contradiction, just an unstated boundary condition. The "general method for deriving a data-driven authority weight without a preset threshold" clause is an interpretive gloss for the research application (not a mathematical claim under test) and follows naturally from the verified mechanics. No credible contradicting source found or expected — this is settled 60+ year old statistical theory. Source quality (a named, single-author academic-department course-notes PDF with a clean, checkable derivation) is adequate for a claim of this type (a well-established textbook derivation, not an extraordinary claim), and I did not rely on the quote alone — I read the surrounding derivation to confirm it's not a misquote or context-stripped fragment.

confirmed.claim: ADWIN (ADaptive WINdowing) uses a variable-size window rather than a fixed one, and detects concept drift by comparing statistics across multiple candidate cut-points within that window rather than on a fixed calendar schedule.
confirmed.quote: a window of variable size while detecting concept drift
confirmed.evidence: The scikit-multiflow ADWIN documentation, when fetched directly, confirms both halves of the claim in its own text: it describes ADWIN as maintaining "a window of variable size while detecting concept drift" (confirms variable-size window vs. fixed) and explicitly states the algorithm analyzes "different cutting points in the sliding window, to verify if there is a significant change in concept," comparing the average of a statistic between the two segments on either side of each cut-point, and discarding data before the point where the difference exceeds a threshold. This is a routine, non-extraordinary description of a well-established, widely-cited algorithm (Bifet & Gavaldà, "Learning from Time-Changing Data with Adaptive Windowing," 2007) that is the standard reference implementation across concept-drift libraries (scikit-multiflow, River, MOA) and has not changed since publication — no fast-moving-field staleness risk. WebSearch was unavailable (session budget exhausted), but the direct WebFetch of the cited source itself independently substantiates the cut-point mechanism that the original supporting quote alone did not cover, and nothing retrieved contradicts or qualifies the claim. Source quality (official library documentation of a peer-reviewed, canonical algorithm) is proportionate to the claim's modest, factual-description strength — this is not an extraordinary or contested claim requiring primary-paper citation.

refuted.claim: Window size (k) selection for concept drift detectors cannot be principled without knowledge of the true underlying data-generating process, meaning the detector's window/lookback parameter is effectively arbitrary rather than derivable from data.

No single preference-elicitation strategy is universally optimal across a conversation — the paper's central motivating finding, directly supporting the doctrine's suspicion that a single fixed (calendar) trigger underperforms adaptive/behavioral triggers.

The signal that should govern strategy switching is dialogue progression / how concrete the user's preference has become, not elapsed time: early stage favors attribute-based elicitation, later stage (as preference becomes concrete) favors item-based elicitation.

Exploration saturation is defined as the point where additional novelty/diversity exposure stops improving utility and can instead reduce engagement or perceived relevance — a candidate behavioral (not calendar-based) signal for when a recommender should stop pushing and potentially re-elicit preferences.

Once a clinician overrode a given alert once, they overrode subsequent repeats of that same alert 87.9% of the time for clinical reminders and 99.9% of the time for drug alerts (DDI/DAI), indicating a near-deterministic habituation/override lock-in after first dismissal.

RL-trained recommenders induce preference shifts that are quantifiably worse (more concentrated/manipulative) than myopic recommenders, which are in turn worse than natural (unintervened) preference evolution — with the paper's proposed metric assigning specific magnitude differences.

The paper's method for defining an 'undesired shift' baseline is to use the preference evolution induced by a random recommender ('natural preference shifts') as the trust-region/safe reference, rather than any calendar-based or fixed schedule.

Saturation shows up as engagement metrics plateauing or becoming unstable rather than as a sharp performance collapse, meaning a drift-detection trigger built on 'metrics dropped sharply' would miss the actual onset and should instead watch for plateau/instability patterns.

The paper's proposed construction method replaces the single 'proportion explained' statistic with two distinct quantities: an individual-level measure of agreement between the surrogate and true endpoint within a single trial, and a trial-level measure of agreement between the treatment effects on the two endpoints across multiple randomized trials — meaning valid surrogate-index construction structurally requires a meta-analytic dataset of several trials, not just one.

The surrogate paradox can occur even when both the treatment-surrogate and surrogate-outcome average causal effects are positive: treatment's average causal effect on the true primary outcome can still be negative, driven by an unmeasured confounder in the absence of directly observed primary-outcome data.

Prentice's classical surrogacy criterion (conditional independence of treatment and primary endpoint given the surrogate) does not, by itself, rule out the surrogate paradox — it 'still lacks transportability' and, along with other classical criteria, 'suffer[s] from the surrogate paradox.'
Across the reviewed literature, alert fatigue is most commonly measured via three metrics: alert quantity, override rate, and acceptance rate (not a frequency-vs-abandonment dose curve).
